replies.author: @Red_Four
replies.text: ​@OldPirate1718I am also a veteran, and that is correct.  Several factors govern how long a person can stay on active duty, but the general rule is that commissioned officers under the rank of brigadier general cannot stay in uniform past 62.  Flag officers must leave service at 64, but the president or secdef/war can allow them to serve until the reach 68.  Other exceptions can be made for chaplains or medical professionals but only up to 68.  The one notable exception is Admiral of the Navies George Dewey who died while still on active duty at the ripe old age of 87.

author: @porksterbob
text:
The revolutionaries are supposed to be unlikeable idiots.  For example, they rob a bank early in the movie and all flee in separate cars that no one has seen them get into. 

They drive quietly and slip away... NO, they speed and crash into other vehicles, slipping across lanes and driving like they are 12 year olds who stole a car. 

That's intentional. 

I feel like the Drinker missed that part of the purpose of the movie was to be a sort of public service announcement to all those on the left who talk like the characters in this movie and suggest some of the same actions. 

Anderson is saying, "This is what you look like, this is what will probably happen, you will achieve none of what you want to achieve if you do it like this."

The only revolutionary who I think the movie respects and that it wants the audience to respect is benicio del Toros character. He is not advocating or commiting violence. He runs a dojo, shelters migrants, and helps his friend, Di caprios character, find his daughter. 

The other revolutionaries are very much meant to be pathetic in their methods.

replies.author: @porksterbob
replies.text:
​@EgertBola Perfidia sexually assaults/humiliates lockjaw when her role was just to neutralize the guard post. 

The only action of substance the French 75 does is to free some of the migrants as part of that first action. Even then, they only bring a single truck so they can't take everyone.  (Despite the migrant camp being right under a public overpass, so counting should have been easy) 

The bank robbery doesn't go well. 

The bombing where they stick bombs in the bathrooms is shown to be inconsequential. Perfidia declares war and speechifies but that's intentionally set against the results of her actions and her circumstances. 

Despite having rhetoric about protecting the downtrodden and her fellow humanity, every action she takes is selfish. The humiliation of lockjaw at the first meeting was her own personal power trip, unlikely to be part of the mission brief. 

When Lockjaw interrupts her placing the bombs, she doesn't tell the rest that she has been compromised and instead goes off for the weirdly coercive one night stand. 

When she has a kid, she puts her own revolutionary cosplay above her husband and child. 

When she gets into witness protection, she names names and gets a nice house. Then decides she doesn't like it and goes to Mexico, never to see or even try to contact her family again. 

The other members of the 75 we see are the radio man who is clearly being allowed to broadcast because what he is saying for the past 15 years doesn't matter. The lady who rescues the daughter is the only one who is competent in the sense that she stays hidden, but she isn't active any more, much like Leo's character.

replies.text:
It didn't seem like that was quite the case, there is nothing that actually condemns the terrorists morally, beyond the law.

You say that they crash because they are supposed to be incompetent idiots that are unlikable. But with that we can Say that Lockjaw was competent and effective, so he was supposed to be likeable? No. He was supposed to be an unlikable person, just like the terrorist black girl he boned. The crash was just a setpiece they used to get the girl in jail. You could change the crash for another setpiece which ends up with the girl in jail, and nothing changes. 

And with dialogue as subtle as the one when Lockjaw got met by two dudes to join their group, as in, subtle as a brick to the face, there was nothing that attempted to paint her in a negative light beyond her own thoughts of jealousy for the baby, which are clearly supposed to make us feel bad for her by the framing and tone of the scene.

This movie did not intend to portray them as incompetent, unlikeable idiots as a warning. It did its best to show them as competent afterwards, with trackers and plans for hiding and bureaucratic phone call codes. Sadly, as pathetic as that method is, it is effective. It's just annoying in the movie. And I Even think the system is validated in the movie after DiCaprio's friend gives him another question/password, the fault líes in DiCaprio for forgetting it for being a druggy.

And if their incompetence was intended, then all subsquent scenes when they are efficient contradict the intention of the writers.

If madame web is funny, but not for the reasons intended by the writers; then this movie's terrorist group is unlikeable, but not for the reasons the writers intended.

And if Anderson is telling both sides "this is what you look like", then he misunderstands and insults both sides by making them as ridiculously exaggerated as possible.

This movie isn't satire, it's parody of the writers imaginary sides.

replies.author: @Argentvs
replies.text:
The hip firing is an actual military technique. Nowadays no longer used but it was real. The US taught it until the 1980s. The jungle units in the Argentine Army kept teaching it up to the 2000s. The position allows for better control of automatic fire in 7.62 guns like the FAL and MAG. It is useful as suppression fire in forested environments. But it is not a regular firing position nor suitable for most normal combat scenarios and it extremely dangerous as it exposes the operator.
It was passed out because armies focused on ammo conservation and burst fire, preferring if possible always range fire. Firing auto at short range must be only if there is no alternative. 
The US MG operators kept using hip firing in close quarter combat in Iraq in example, sweeping inside houses.

author: @reddherod7677
text: Spoilers:  I think an important aspect is that the mother doesn't get to re-unite with her family or is shown to have been right all along.  She represents the wholly incorrect extremist view of her own ideology where she was willing to abandon her family for ego and rat on all her friends because she was a coward.  She's never forgiven or rewarded for that.  In fact, every member of her team that's shown to be more interested in the performative aspects of rebellion are either killed (like the 'my p*ssy is power' bank robber lady) or mocked as entirely ineffective (the triggered dispatch operator for the rebel guys).  In the end, the daughter states that she's headed to a protest.  She's not bringing guns or bombs.  Also, the film includes minority characters but doesn't lionize them (the daughter's non-binary friend is the one who betrays her info to the cops) and the actual soldiers under Lockjaw's command are not portrayed as racist, evil or overly violent - they do their jobs, don't abuse civilians and, at worst, taze DiCaprio.  The movie wasn't perfect for me but it is a film that's willing to show that a pregnant, black freedom-fighter black woman can be a flawed, selfish and ultimately non-redeemed character in the narrative while also being a caution about how performative, violent and entitled activism isn't heroic or worth celebrating.

replies.author: @reddherod7677
replies.text: @ZombiiChix I think the difference comes down to the fact that the daughter leaves to join a meeting place for protestors, not anarchists.  She's not bringing guns or bombs.  She's not fighting the way her mother thought the battle should be fought AKA with indiscriminate violence.  She's taking a different approach but she's not abandoning the issues she cares about.  I don't get the sense that she has the vanity, impatience and recklessness that her mother had and therefore the moment of catharsis she gets reading the letter comes from familial longing rather than radicalizing rhetoric.

replies.author: @zachrincon327
replies.text: ​@fu102 out of every character in the movie the only ones who I'd argue are outright sympathetic or even fully likable are the daughter and Benicio's character and that's probably it. Leo's character is the most complex on how you're supposed to feel about him. However after the time jump he spends the rest of his time trying to distance himself from his political extremism days to protect his daughter. He's sort of the embodiment of the idea of "the older you get, the more conservative you become". One of the things I admire greatly about this film is that it shows how having a child genuinely changes someone. Especially Leo and Teyana Taylor's characters. Leo immediately drops the whole revolutionary act and becomes a doting father while Teyana Taylor's character succumbs to post-partum depression. She keeps distancing herself from those who care about her and it costs her her freedom while Leo leaves that all behind to become a father. One thing I find interesting is the contrast between Leo and Sean Penn's character. One is running away from their political extremist past while the other in Sean Penn is desperately sprinting towards it with The Christmas Adventurers Club, a club that wants nothing to do with him because of his own insecurities and who he actually is. It's that desperate chase for acceptance in a club, same as Leo with the French 75 before he had his daughter. It all concludes perfectly with that letter from Teyana's character to her daughter about how she and Leo didn't change the world but only made it harder for her and that she hopes she can be better than they were. Leading to that ending where it's up to his daughter to make her own decisions on who she wants to be (we don't see the protest she's attending at the end for this reason, it reads to me more as letting your kids be their own person and to be better than you, because they will be in some aspects). Paul Thomas Anderson masterfully cuts through the politics of it all and finds the humanity lying within his characters, even the worst ones, and to me that's beautiful.

author: @patchacuti7338
text:
I think the point of the movie was to highlight how love is stronger than hate. I think the reason that people don't like this movie is because the protagonists are not the heroes of the movie. They are the heroes of their own lives. Heroes to the ones they love. For example, willa is initially only trying to save herself, but at the end of the movie she decides that, despite all the pain it has brought her and the fact that they clearly did it the wrong way, the battles that her parents fought for **were** worth fighting for. Another reason i think people don't like this movie is because it is neither a good vs evil, nor a lesser evil vs a greater evil type of movie. there are at least 3 clear antagonists in the movie, and the first half the movie really doesn't have protagonists at all. Bob is morally all over the place but you can tell that he truly loves his daughter. Willa and Sensei Sergio are the two most clear protagonists as one is fighting for her own survival and the second is simply doing all he can to help others. It may not be as satisfying to watch, but it's a story worth telling. In real life, the line between good and evil is a lot less clear. I think this movie does a great job of humanizing flawed characters with good intentions, and showing the true evil nature of the groupthink that is demonstrated by the revolutionary group. How easily something that begins as fight against evil can become evil in and of itself and defeat it's own purpose. I think the writing is actually quite good compared to a lot of what is coming out of hollywood, and the cinematography is excellent. This is far removed from all the ridiculous shallow-depth-of-field-in-every-shot movies that feel so bland and uninspired that even with good writing they fall flat for just not standing out at all visually. This movie brings us into the world of the movie and lets the natural backgrounds and landscapes of the sets tell the story rather than green screen some random cgi bullshit into the background. I don't think it's the best movie every made, but c'mon, it's pretty damn good.

Take all of this with a grain of salt because I watched An Elephant Sitting still by Hu Bo twice, so you know the type of slow burns into absolutely nothing I am capable of enjoying.

​@daveh322I have an Unlimited Pass from Regal Cinemas, so for $24.99 a month I see all the movies I want at no additional cost, they just have to be more than 90 minutes apart. 

The silver screen is like a temple to me. It's a quiet place of reflection free from outside noise where I can go for a couple or a few hours and let myself become emerged in a story, in the scenery, and in the score for a while without feeling beholden to work, or to checking my phone, or to any of the other things going on in my normal day to day life. 

It's meditation, and that's why I go. For me. Not for Hollywood.
